speaker: interviewer
text: Today [BACKROUND NOISE] is August 7, 1996. I'm interviewing Michael Lipenholtz. My name is Judy Sukenik. The interview was being conducted in Brooklyn, New York in the United States of America in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of times in Bialystok, [INAUDIBLE] a Jewish city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 7, 1996. I am interviewing Michael Lipenholtz. My name is Judy Sukenik, S-U-K-E-N-I-K. The interview is being conducted in Brooklyn, New York in the United States of America in English. Please introduce yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Michael Lipenholtz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-P-E-N-H-O-L-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael, Mikhoel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lipenholtz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was then in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, it was a Russian, and Lithuanian. And they used to fight over it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birthday? Was it November?

speaker: interviewee
text: November. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] November. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: That makes you how old today?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old it makes me today? [LAUGHS] I forgot. I don't think-- I got a wife-- I'm not allowed to talk, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you remember back to when you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were things like? Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I always went to school. It wasn't such a thing, like no school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name it used to-- they used to call then, Gymnasium [NON-ENGLISH] Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to elementary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what is the elementary school, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Hebrew school. Real Hebrew, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live? In

speaker: interviewee
text: The street? [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH] A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who lived there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia, Shaina.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bisciks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else live with you in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '11, I think, '11, 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to be in business. And then later on I remember after the-- the Depression, he had to come-- worked with his mother-- with my mother in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of store was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A grocery store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] What-- I was-- sometimes I was helping them. You know, on-- on Sunday, you had to have your stores closed. You're not allowed to have them open. So they used to do the business through the back store. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was on a Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you helped with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I-- on Saturday, on Saturday, they were sleeping or resting or do something. I was standing over there on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the family's religious affiliation? Were you orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father go to shul on Shabbos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Shabbos like at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos was a real Shabbos. Nothing doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I eat Friday night?

speaker: interviewer
text: The meal, what was Shabbos like? Did you have a challah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Challah they used bake themselves. My mother used to come home and bake-- bake the challah. They [INAUDIBLE]-- everybody had a maid. If you worked in-- in any place, you had to have a maid. Because a maid used to cost you 20 zlotys. 20 zlotys was $5 a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your maid's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Rosalia. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wasn't Jewish. She used to take me to church [LAUGHS] on Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When we find out, they had to-- had to fire the maid. But she took me every time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what you told me she had put on your neck?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And when they found out, they fired her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get a different maid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had to have a maid. We couldn't have it without the maid, you know. But you had to-- to cook for Shabbos. They used to-- they didn't trust the maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who did the cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother used to cook, come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to gefilte fish for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I didn't eat it. But I didn't like fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have chicken or meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, chicken, meat, and everything. 'Cause, no problem the food, by us anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father make kiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have guests for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would come?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had friends most of the time. Because my father was born-- oh, boy-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. This was a city not far from Vilna, a mid-sized city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to come to Vilna. They used to come to Chaim Lipenholtz. They used to sleep-- we had a special place for-- for-- for company. What did your house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had an apartment. [INAUDIBLE] a four-room apartment, I think. And once we had a five room apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you hear any news on the radio about what was happening around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sitting in the front of the store. It was big street, you know, like. And all of a sudden, somebody hit me like with a hammer, you know. And I-- I-- and I was stand up. And I was looking, like didn't see who it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this wasn't Jewish. You see, they hated the Jews. That's what it was. And I was always with-- with them. I went to the army, I was the one. Jew in this-- in this part, you know, in the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, how they call it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to called it [NON-ENGLISH], you know. [NON-ENGLISH], it was like a-- for-- for-- anyway, I was one Jew in this place. I was lucky, because there was another guy from-- from-- from Poland, you know, from far Poland, from the back. They speak-- spoke a good Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I looked like a goy. And I-- so they didn't know. Who is the Jew? I went and-- and then I went to the school [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of [INAUDIBLE]-- how is it? When you finished, you became a sergeant or something like this. Engineer Engineer. Was it engineering school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This wasn't engineer. Only school. Military school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, sergeant. Hm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they hate the Jews. And lately, before the war if you had finished school with-- you could get in in college. if they could do it. They wouldn't take no Jewish at all. They wouldn't take Jewishes in the army. This was the-- they didn't talk about it. But they didn't took it. But the guys who had the schooling, were lucky. But they didn't take them into their army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what your mother looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a young boy, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a young girl, a healthy one. That's it. My father used to go to shul every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother go to shul on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos? I think so, maybe not. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember any yontif at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can your remember Pesach at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Pesach, was a seder. Sure. This was normal. It wasn't a holiday, you know, like to make a-- because this was like this. Pesach was real Pescach. We used to buy-- bake the matzoh. We had to go to the bakery. There used to be a bakery. And they made it kosher for Pesach. And you used to bring the flour to bake the matzoh. And you were waiting and watching.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had to have it put it in the big-- big what was it? What do they call it? I forgot. Oven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not oven, after. From the oven into the basket, but it was a big basket like this. And then you take it and carry it home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did every family bake their own matzohs?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bakery, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you make your own wine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I forgot about the wine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did you do it? Do you know how to do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. We used to buy grapes and let them for ferment. And then you had to squeeze it, and I-- I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody. [LAUGHTER] My father was the man doer. And we used to make [INAUDIBLE]. You know what [INAUDIBLE] is? We used to make [INAUDIBLE], too. It tasted good, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you still remember the taste?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you steal the afikoman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. What's the matter with you? You're asking me such questions. [LAUGHTER] I was specialist. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And not get any special present for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to ask for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you ask for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ask-- I don't know I asked. I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm-hmm. What did you do when you got together with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used-- we used to be boys, you know. We used-- our friends were Jewish. And every had- everybody had a Jewish education. And we were happy. Do you know who was my rabbi?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] You hear about him?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was the rabbi from Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember him. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what that means? They used to come from all the world he should [NON-ENGLISH]. That's the way there. And he was-- he was the rabbi. [BACKROUND NOISE] But he was lucky. When the Germans got in, he died right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- when he was working in the street, he was working with [INAUDIBLE]. You know? He was an old man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the [NON-ENGLISH] in your shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? [INAUDIBLE] Koussevitzki. Josef.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josef Koussevitzki?

speaker: interviewer
text: Moshe. Moshe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe Koussevitzki, that's right. He-- he started over in my shul. He started like a-- we were the third of the fourth biggest shul in Vilna. And we had a lot of shuls, in the hundreds. And he was started [INAUDIBLE] [NON-ENGLISH]. And they chose him. And he was a lot of years over there. And from-- and from there I think he went to the Dutch shul, you know, the biggest. And from there, they took him to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] was the name. And the guy who built the shul, the rich guy, his name was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the shul look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: A nice shul, a real nice shul even here in the United States with tall ceilings, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the women sit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Women were sitting-- if they had a balcony, they were in the balcony. If they hadn't a balcony, they had to sit in the back. I'm sorry. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did you have newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a lot of newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's happening? We knew everything. This was real thing. [INAUDIBLE] maybe Vilna, like this, Jewish newspapers, had maybe six, seven newspapers, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a telephone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not-- not anybody had a telephone. Telephone are for doctors. And who needed a telephone? You know, for bankers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations when you were a young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was the only one. You know why? Because I-- I-- I wasn't looking for girls. [LAUGHTER] I had a lot of girls. And I didn't [INAUDIBLE] to. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got together with your friends, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the movies. We went to the games.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play soccer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I play soccer. And I-- I have boxing, too. When the Germans came and they moved the cadenza from the wall-- you know, they were looking for something. They say, who was living here a boxed? It was so many boxing gloves over there. [LAUGHS] Really. I'm-- I'm telling just the truth here now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your life good?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good for me. I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, became worse. Later on, you couldn't get a job. I had a brother five years older. He finished gymnasium and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe. And he couldn't get a Job for nothing, for no money. A Jew couldn't get a job. And I grow up in meantime, and I see what's going to happen. No jobs. You can have a job maybe to be something. But it's not the job, you know. So I decided myself-- because it was you had to finish gymnasium. The rest wasn't so important by-- by a Jew, you know. In Vilna, we had a technical school. It was famous in the United States. Because they paid for it, for the school. [LAUGHS] From-- they were [INAUDIBLE] like to the [INAUDIBLE] or something like this. And I finished that. I had to go to the gymnasium. My father wanted me to go over there. The-- the price was the same almost every place. So I-- I decide I'll go to the technical school, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was-- but I was there three years. And I finished. But I got a job right away. I had a job in-- what was it? [INAUDIBLE] they used to call it, a famous radio factory in the world. So I got the job there. What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a technician over there, you know. But I knew everything, you know, mechanical. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a job. And then I went to work for another job. And I worked two jobs. And it wasn't hard for me. I don't know what it was there. I was enjoying it. But the meantime, I had two jobs. And all of a sudden, I used to make money from both sides. And I live in the big house, you know, in the big house in Vilna. We had an elevator. This was the second elevator [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] in Vilna. The first one was belonged to the transportation employment, you know. It belonged to the government.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had the first one. And we had the second. This was six stories. We had six. And they had seven. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And after the war, I took over the-- the first one. I took it over for the-- for the [INAUDIBLE] you know. I knew where it is. And I knew all the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that you were in the Polish cavalry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't take too many Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe they liked me. [LAUGHTER] I mean it. This was a hard job.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you join the cavalry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what-- what was it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was like a year before the war. You see, from over there they took us from Vilna down to Poland over there. And it started, the war started. We didn't know-- we didn't know nothing. We didn't have no radio in the-- in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll tell you something about the army. Do you want to know? [LAUGHS] Nothing good. [LAUGHS] In the army, they took us over there. And right away I something anti-Semitism, you know? They hate Jews. It was in their-- in their nature, in the blood. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the-- after the first or second day, they wake us up in the middle of the night. What happened? The guy, you know, the corporal took him. This was to take-- take something from the soldiers, you know, to break him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He thought-- he could break his leg, he says. It was a piece of straw like this. And he could break his leg [INAUDIBLE]. So they wake us up. And [INAUDIBLE] everybody up, you know, in-- in-- had a-- they didn't get no [INAUDIBLE]. Had something wrong with us, you know. We had to take brushes and clean the-- the floors, everything. We-- but the guys, the rest of it, not the Jews, they didn't have. They had some-- some brushes, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they used to clean it with the-- with [INAUDIBLE]. I had-- I had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a shoe brush, you know, to-- to brush the shoes. Well, of course, I was doing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we started together, you know, put the-- the dirt maybe so much there together. And we had to pull it in the pail. So one of the guys took some dirt, put it over my head, you know. I was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know what happened. I got up. And I had the brush in my hand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I broke his nose. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the first thing right away. So I had respect. All of a sudden, I became one of the boys, you know. There had a lot of respect for me. But they didn't put me in jail for breaking a nose. It was really [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it was before you would swear to the-- to the constitutionals or somebody over there that you obey all the laws. And then-- then they can take you to the-- to the court and put you in jail. But it was before. So I was lucky they didn't put me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that you had a horse when you were in the cavalry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody had a horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] is a-- a gypsy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. And what happened to your horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to everybody? But-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So how--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the horses to the war, we put us-- they put us in train, five or six horses in a train. And one guy who had to clean them, you know, from the-- and they took us down to Poland, you know, down. Down to, like-- not far from the Romanian or something. frontier. And they let us out from the trains. And we started-- they started bombarding us. We didn't know nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They were already the Germans there. And we had the horses. And they had the-- the-- what do you call it? The tanks. So what should we do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, all the officers vanished. No officers. Nobody should take us, tell us what to do. And we-- we had horses. We didn't have no food. This was the Polish army. Help me. And then we had to go. We didn't have to-- we didn't know where to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were looking for the-- for the officers. I went with another guy. He says, [NON-ENGLISH]. Means, if somebody goes after us, they shoot us. And then through the night, they disappeared. This was not just from a-- from a-- 1,000 men. This was for 5,000 men. They disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your cavalry?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to your cavalry? Everybody for himself. This-- this was far. And we didn't know what to do. We didn't know that the war was almost finished. In a couple of days, the war was finished. And the Polish vanished. They-- most of the other officers, there went to Romania. You know about Romania. They went to Romania. And we didn't know nothing, where to go or what. We didn't have nothing to eat. They left everybody like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had to go-- everybody had to go-- we're going home? That's what we were thinking. Where should we go? We-- we started. And then they started collecting the Poles, Polish soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to a place. We were over-- over water or something. And over there, all of a sudden we see Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? They divided Poland between the Germans and the Russians. And we went to the East. Because Vilna was to the East, you know. And then you had to go north. And all of a sudden, the Russian came in. We didn't know nothing about it. We'll continue on the next tape.
